frames.description: the ground truth — what actually happened on the ridge
what Wrenna Syle believes about the state of the cut, from the word relayed to her

branches.description: Bo sends the cart to hold the lower edge — the town lives
Bo keeps the cart with the saws — the fire jumps and the town is lost

entities.description: Bo Aldercott, the town's road-warden, leads the cut crew
Wrenna Syle, the only one who reads fire well enough to walk a backfire down a slope; leads the burn crew
Tam, the runner who carries word of the cut's progress between the two crews
the relief hand-team that closes the granite gap by pick and bar
the single mule-drawn water bowser — the only water on the ridge
the firebreak line being sawed and cleared along the saddle
the controlled backfire Wrenna walks down toward the cut
the two-week wildfire cresting the ridge
Coldwater Bend, in the valley below the ridge
the western draw, where a granite seam stalls the cut

predicates.description: the state of the firebreak line at the western draw: open / closed / breached
which crew-lead the one water cart is with

facts.claim: The two-week wildfire crests Mara Ridge above Coldwater Bend on the night the valley wind will reverse and drive it downslope at dawn
The only play before the wind turns is to cut the saddle firebreak and walk a controlled backfire down so the two fires meet at the cut line and die for want of fuel
Bo Aldercott's cut crew opens the firebreak at the east end of the saddle and saws west
One mule-drawn water cart is the only water on the ridge, and both crews will need it through the night
Wrenna Syle lights the backfire high on the slope and begins walking its lower edge down toward the saddle
How fast Wrenna may walk the backfire depends minute by minute on how far Bo's cut has actually reached, or it crosses the unfinished line into the town
Tam runs the line between the crews, carrying word of the cut's progress so Wrenna can pace the backfire; neither lead can see the other in the dark and smoke
The saws strike a granite seam at the western draw and the firebreak stalls there, leaving the line open
The night turns on the one cart: the saw-teams need it to keep cutting in the heat while the burn crew needs it to hold the backfire's lower edge; for the first hour it stays with the cut crew
Wrenna believes the western-draw line has already been cut and closed
The word relayed to Wrenna garbled: the western draw was reported lined when the granite gap is in fact still open
Trusting the report that the line is closed, Wrenna quickens the backfire's descent toward the saddle
At midnight a relief runner reaches Bo with the truth: the western-draw gap is still open and the quickened backfire's lower edge is coming down on it
The cart call is forced — there is water for only one place and the dawn wall is fixed: hold the lower edge, or keep cutting the granite
Bo sends the cart down to hold the backfire's lower edge at the draw and pulls a hand-team to take the granite gap by pick and bar, where no saws and no water are needed
With the cart on it, the backfire's lower edge is held at the draw for the crucial minutes instead of jumping the open line
The hand-team closes the granite gap by pick and bar; the western-draw line is shut at last
Held back just enough, the backfire arrives at the now-closed line; the two fires meet at the cut and die for want of anything left to burn
By the time the fires meet, the line Wrenna had been told was closed is at last actually closed — her wrong belief has been overtaken by the truth
The wind turns at dawn: the cut is complete, the backfire met the line, and neither crew failed for want of water at the wrong moment — Coldwater Bend wakes to ash on the ridge and nothing more
Bo keeps the cart with the saws at the draw to beat the granite faster, trusting the backfire's lower edge to hold dry a little longer
Dry on its lower edge and quickened by Wrenna's pace, the backfire jumps the still-open draw before the gap is cut and crosses the unfinished line
The crossed fire runs downslope toward Coldwater Bend; the saws never beat the granite in time to matter
The wind turns at dawn and drives the loosed fire, already below the saddle, down onto the town; Coldwater Bend is gone by full light
